nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated sandy and rubble flats adjacent to coral reefs, generally at 3–40 m depth; constructs and maintains vertical burrows in mixed sand and crushed-coral substrate, hovering vertically above the burrow opening

decorPreferences: deep sand bed of mixed grades (8–15 cm) blended with crushed coral and small rubble for burrow construction
various small rocks and shell fragments to reinforce burrow walls
live rock perimeter set on the bottom glass (rock should rest on glass, not on sand, so burrows can't undermine it)
open sandy footprint in the foreground
tight-fitting lid or canopy — non-negotiable due to high jumping risk

diet.notes: Primarily a zooplanktivore in the wild, hovering vertically above the burrow to pick passing zooplankton from the water column. Has been documented accepting mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, small pieces of mussel and clam, bloodworms, Calanus, and prepared frozen marine carnivore mixes. Generally advised to feed small meaty portions two to three times daily; food is often best directed near the burrow opening initially, as newly-introduced specimens tend to be shy feeders that will not compete well against faster tankmates.

avoidWith: fast aggressive feeders that monopolize food
other burrowing fish that may displace it
very small ornamental shrimp (may be eaten)

summary: A peaceful Caribbean burrowing fish with a bright yellow head and pale blue-green body that hovers vertically above its sand burrow. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly aside from a notable jumping risk that requires a tight-fitting lid.
careNotes:
The Yellowhead Jawfish is one of the most popular and widely-kept jawfish species due to its peaceful temperament, modest tank-size requirement, and engaging burrowing behavior. It is native to the Western Central Atlantic from southern Florida and the Bahamas through the Caribbean to northern South America, occupying sandy and rubble zones adjacent to coral reefs at roughly 3–40 m depth.

Temperature: sources span a notable range. LiveAquaria and AquaticCommunity suggest 72–78°F (~22–26°C); FishBase reports a wild range of 22–28°C with a preferred range of 24.7–28°C; FishLore and other hobby sources cite 75–82°F (~24–28°C). The 23–27°C range used here is a cross-source average and should suit most aquarium setups; the species tolerates tropical temperatures well, distinguishing it from its cool-water cousin the Blue Dot Jawfish (Opistognathus rosenblatti) which requires a chiller.

Adult size: maximum total length is reliably reported as ~10 cm (about 4 inches); LiveAquaria notes wild specimens may reach 5 inches (12.7 cm) while aquarium specimens tend to top out closer to 4 inches.

Substrate is the most important husbandry detail: the species is a dedicated burrow-builder requiring a deep sand bed (generally advised at 8–15 cm) blended with crushed coral and small shells/rubble so the burrow walls remain structurally sound. Rocks should rest on the tank's bottom glass rather than directly on the sand, because the fish will undermine sand-supported rockwork through burrowing.

Escape risk is high — Yellowhead Jawfish are widely reported jumpers, especially when startled or newly introduced. A tight-fitting lid is essential.

Temperament is peaceful toward most tankmates, though they may be bullied by aggressive feeders or larger burrowing fish. Multiple specimens can be kept in a sufficiently large aquarium with enough territory for each individual to claim its own burrow site, and pairs of Yellowhead Jawfish often form stable monogamous bonds in the wild and in captivity. The species is generally considered reef-safe; isolated reports describe nipping at very small ornamental shrimp.

Breeding has been documented in captivity. Males are paternal mouthbrooders, incubating a sticky egg mass in the buccal cavity until the larvae hatch.

Feeding can be slow at first — newly-introduced specimens tend to stay within the burrow until comfortable, and food is best directed near the burrow opening for the first weeks. Once established, they readily accept a range of frozen and prepared carnivore foods.
breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder. Pairs form readily in captivity; the female deposits a sticky egg mass that the male incubates in his mouth until hatching (commonly reported around 7–10 days). Captive spawning has been documented at several public aquariums and by advanced hobbyists. Breeding difficulty is generally considered moderate-to-advanced because raising the small pelagic larvae through metamorphosis requires copepod-grade live foods and very stable water quality.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source from manifest. Detail page at https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/yellowhead-jawfish supplied tank minimum (30 gal / ~114 L), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025), aquarium-adult size (~4 in / 10 cm), diet, peaceful temperament, reef-safe rating, and substrate/lid recommendations.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Secondary source from manifest. Confirmed taxonomy (Opistognathidae), max length 10 cm TL, depth range 3–40 m, Western Central Atlantic distribution (Florida/Bahamas to Barbados and northern South America), reef-associated burrowing habitat, trophic level 3.0 (carnivore/planktivore), preferred temperature 24.7–28°C, monogamous home-ranging pairs, and male paternal mouthbrooding behavior.

additional.name: FishLore
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (30 gal / ~114 L), 3-foot minimum length, temperature 75–82°F, lifespan (5+ years), 3–5 in sand bed depth, and care-level rating (easy-to-moderate). Temperature range is wider/warmer than LiveAquaria — averaged across sources for final value.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Confirmed 30-gallon minimum, ~4 in max aquarium size, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, jumping risk requiring tight lid, deep sand + coral rubble substrate, and feeding behavior (will not compete with aggressive feeders).

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Florida to northern South America), 30-gal/3-ft minimum tank, 7.5 cm+ sand depth, water parameters (75–82°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.023), peaceful temperament, and paternal mouthbrooding.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder; pairs form in captivity; egg mass incubated ~7–10 days. Raising pelagic larvae through metamorphosis requires copepod-grade live foods and stable water quality.
